subject: Physics
q: A carrier wave of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used for transmitting a message signal. The peak voltage of the modulating signal given to achieve a modulation index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> will be:
question: A carrier wave of peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>14</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used for transmitting a message signal. The peak voltage of the modulating signal given to achieve a modulation index of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>80</mn><mo>%</mo></math> will be:
solution: Modulation index, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>c</mi></msub></mfrac></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>⇒</mo><msub><mi>A</mi><mi>m</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.8</mn><mi> </mi><mo>×</mo><mn>14</mn></math><math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>11</mn><mo>.2</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>

subject: Physics
q: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
question: A signal of 5 kHz frequency is amplitude modulated on a carrier wave of frequency 2 MHz. The frequencies of the resultant signal is/are:
solution:
Frequency of signal wave =5 kHz=f. Carrier wave frequency =2MHz

=2000kHz=fc. The resultant signal maximum frequency=f+fc

= 5 + 2 0 0 0 kHz

= 2 0 0 5 kHz

Resultant signal minimum frequency

=fc-f

= 2 0 0 0 - 5 kHz

= 1995 kHz

subject: Physics
q: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
question: Which of the following statement is NOT correct?
options: Ground wave signals are more stable than the sky wave signals.
The critical frequency of an ionospheric layer is the highest frequency that will be reflected back by the layer when it is vertically incident.
Electromagnetic waves of frequencies higher than about $30 \mathrm{MHz}$ cannot penetrate the ionosphere.
Sky wave signals in the broadcast frequency range are stronger at night than in the day time.
solution: Above critical frequency $\left(f_c\right)$, an electromagnetic wave penetrates the ionosphere and is not reflected by it.

subject: Physics
q: For an amplitude modulated wave the minimum amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, while the modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The maximum amplitude of the modulated wave is:
question: For an amplitude modulated wave the minimum amplitude is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, while the modulation index is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>%</mo></math>. The maximum amplitude of the modulated wave is:
solution:
Modulatio index is the ratio of the difference in amplitudes to the sum of the amplitudes.

The formula to calculate the modulation index of a wave is given by

μ=Amax-AminAmax+Amin   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required value of maximum amplitude.

0.6=Amax-3Amax+3⇒0.6Amax+1.8=Amax-3⇒0.4Amax=4.8⇒Amax= 4.80.4=12 V

question: The wavelength of the carrier waves in a modern optical fiber communication network is close to:
solution: For minimum attenuation, the wavelength of carrier waves is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>

subject: Physics
q: A massage signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>  is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
question: A massage signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>  is used to modulate a carrier signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi></math>. The bandwidth of the amplitude modulated wave is
solution:
The relation between bandwidth and the signal frequency is given by

Bandwidth=2× signal frequency   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameter into equation (1) to calculate the required bandwidth.

Bandwidth= 2 × 3 kHz= 6 kHz

subject: Physics
q: A sinusoidal wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by another sinusoidal wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1000</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The amplitude of minimum frequency component of modulated signal is
question: A sinusoidal wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>40</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>10</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is amplitude modulated by another sinusoidal wave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mfenced><mi>t</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>20</mn><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1000</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The amplitude of minimum frequency component of modulated signal is
solution:
Here, modulation index, μ=AmAc=2040=12

Modulated amplitude =μAc2

=12×402=10

subject: Physics
q:
We do not transmit low frequency signal to long distances because
(a) The size of the antenna should be comparable to signal wavelength which is unreal solution for a signal of longer wavelength.
(b) Effective power radiated by a long wavelength baseband signal would be high.
(c) We want to avoid mixing up signals transmitted by different transmitter simultaneously.
(d) Low frequency signal can be sent to long distances by superimposing with a high frequency wave as well.

Therefore, the most suitable option will be :
question:
We do not transmit low frequency signal to long distances because
(a) The size of the antenna should be comparable to signal wavelength which is unreal solution for a signal of longer wavelength.
(b) Effective power radiated by a long wavelength baseband signal would be high.
(c) We want to avoid mixing up signals transmitted by different transmitter simultaneously.
(d) Low frequency signal can be sent to long distances by superimposing with a high frequency wave as well.

Therefore, the most suitable option will be :
solution:
For longer wavelength, size of antenna would increase. The size of the antennas should be atleast λ4 for high efficiency of signal radiation. 

The effective power radiated by an antenna is inversely proportional to the square of the wavelength, so, for the same antenna length, the power radiated increases with decreasing wavelength or increasing frequency.Hence, the effective power radiated by a long-wavelength baseband signal would be small.

Also, mixing of signals needs to be avoided, as all the signals get mixed together and a receiver can not separate them from each other.  Also, we can use modulation to send low frequency signal by superimposing them with high frequency signals. Electrical audio signals are superimposed on a high frequency wave called the carrier wave. The carrier wave is generated from radio-frequency oscillators and is undamped in nature.

subject: Physics
q:
The height of a transmitting antenna at the top of a tower is 25 m and that of receiving antenna is, 49 m. The maximum distance between them, for satisfactory communication in LOS (Line-Of-Sight) is K5×102 m. The value of K is _____ .

(Assume radius of Earth is 64×105 m) [Calculate upto nearest integer value]
question:
The height of a transmitting antenna at the top of a tower is 25 m and that of receiving antenna is, 49 m. The maximum distance between them, for satisfactory communication in LOS (Line-Of-Sight) is K5×102 m. The value of K is _____ .

(Assume radius of Earth is 64×105 m) [Calculate upto nearest integer value]
solution:
Here, 

hT=49m, hR=25m, R=64×105m

Maximum distance between two antennae is,

d=2RhT+2RhR=2R49+2R25=122R=122×64×105=1925×102 m

subject: Physics
q: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: The required height of a TV tower which can cover the population of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>03</mn></math> lakh is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math>. If the average population density is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo></math>per square <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi></math> and the radius of earth is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Maximum range of TV transmission d=2Rh

d=2×6400×h×10-3 (here, h in m)

Maximum area of TV transmission

Area =πd2

=π×2×6400×h×10-3 km2

Now, 6.03×100000=100×π×2×6400×10-3 h

h=6.03×10510×π×128

h=150 m

subject: Physics
q: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
question: In a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CE</mi></math> transistor amplifier, the audio signal voltage across the collector resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math>, if the base resistance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kΩ</mi></math> and the current amplification of the transistor is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> then the input signal voltage is
solution:
Current amplification factor,

β=ΔICΔIB

Collector current,

ΔIC=2V2×103Ω=1×10-3 A

Base current,

ΔIB=VBRB=VB1×103=VB×10-3

Given, β=100

Now, 100=10-3VB×10-3

⇒VB=1100 V=10 mV

subject: Physics
q: In the case of amplitude modulation to avoid distortion the modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>μ</mi></mfenced></math> should be :
question: In the case of amplitude modulation to avoid distortion the modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>μ</mi></mfenced></math> should be :
solution:
The modulation index of an amplitude modulated signal is defined as the measure or extent of amplitude variation about an un-modulated carrier.

Modulation index is  μ=AmAc

To avoid distortion, μ≤1 because  μ>1 will result in interference between career frequency and message frequency, resulting into distortion.

subject: Physics
q: An antenna is mounted on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall building. What will be the wavelength of signal that can be radiated effectively by the transmission tower upto a range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ?
question: An antenna is mounted on a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> tall building. What will be the wavelength of signal that can be radiated effectively by the transmission tower upto a range of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>44</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> ?
solution:
d=2RhT

⇒44×103=2×6400×103×hT

⇒hT=151.25 m

∵ lantenna≥λ4

⇒λ≤4l

considering, hT=1= length of antenna

λ=4×151.25=605 m

subject: Physics
q:
An antenna is placed in a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 6.25. If the maximum size of that antenna is 5.0 mm, it can radiate a signal of minimum frequency of _____ GHz.

 (Given μr=1 for dielectric medium)
question:
An antenna is placed in a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 6.25. If the maximum size of that antenna is 5.0 mm, it can radiate a signal of minimum frequency of _____ GHz.

 (Given μr=1 for dielectric medium)
solution:
We know that, refractive index is related to velocity as n=cv.

Here, n=εrμr=6.251=2.5

Since, effective length of antenna l>λ4⇒l>v4f

So, f>v4l=c4l2.5=3×1082.5×4×5×10-3=6×109 Hz=6 GHz.

subject: Physics
q: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
question: To double the covering range of a TV transmitting tower, its height should be multiplied by:
solution:
The range of a tower in the communication system is given by

Range=2hR

Here, h= Height of the tower,

R= radius of Earth

Given, The range of tower is to be doubled.

As, Range ∝h, to make the range double, the height h have to be made 4 times.

subject: Physics
q: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
question: The number of amplitude modulated broadcast stations that can be accommodated in a $300 \mathrm{kHz}$ band width for the highest modulating frequency $15 \mathrm{kHz}$ will be:
solution:
Given, modulating frequency $f_m$ $=15 \mathrm{KHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ Bandwidth of one channel $=2 f_m=30 \mathrm{kHz}$
$\therefore \quad$ No of channels accommodate
$$
=\frac{300 \mathrm{kHz}}{30 \mathrm{kHz}}=10
$$

subject: Physics
q: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
question: A radar has a power of $1 \mathrm{~Kw}$ and is operating at a frequency of $10 \mathrm{~GHz}$. It is located on a mountain top of height $500 \mathrm{~m}$. The maximum distance upto which it can detect object located on the surface of the earth (Radius of earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ ) is
solution: Maximum distance on earth where object can be detected is $\mathrm{d}$, then $\begin{array}{ll} & (h+R)^2=d^2+R^2 \\ \Rightarrow & d^2=h^2+2 R h \\ \text { since } & h < < R, \quad \Rightarrow d^2=2 h R \\ \Rightarrow & d=\sqrt{2(500)\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)}=80 \mathrm{~km} \end{array}$

subject: Physics
q: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
question: A baseband signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>MHz</mi></math> frequency is modulated with a carrier signal of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>GHz</mi></math> frequency using amplitude modulation method. What should be the minimum size of antenna required to transmit the modulated signal ?
solution:
The wavelength of the carrier wave is,

λc=cνc=3×1083.5×109=335 m.

The minimum size of the antenna required is 14th of the wavelength of the carrier. Therefore,

l=14×335=21.4 mm

subject: Physics
q:
Given the electric field of a complete amplitude modulated wave as
$$
\vec{E}=\hat{i} E_c\left(1+\frac{E_m}{E_c} \cos \omega_m t\right) \cos \omega_c t
$$
Where the subscript c stands for the carrier wave and $\mathrm{m}$ for the modulating signal. The frequencies present in the modulated wave are
question:
Given the electric field of a complete amplitude modulated wave as
$$
\vec{E}=\hat{i} E_c\left(1+\frac{E_m}{E_c} \cos \omega_m t\right) \cos \omega_c t
$$
Where the subscript c stands for the carrier wave and $\mathrm{m}$ for the modulating signal. The frequencies present in the modulated wave are
solution:
The frequencies present in amplitude modulated wave are :
Carrier frequency $=\omega_c$
Upper side band frequency $=\omega_c+\omega_m$
Lower side band frequency $=\omega_c-\omega_m$.

subject: Physics
q: A message signal of frequency <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used execute amplitude modulation on a carrier wave of frequency <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mi> </mi><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The modulation index and difference between the two side band frequencies are:
question: A message signal of frequency <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is used execute amplitude modulation on a carrier wave of frequency <math><mn>300</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">G</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math> and peak voltage <math><mi> </mi><mn>400</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> . The modulation index and difference between the two side band frequencies are:
solution:
Modulating index is given by,
μ=AmAe
=100 V400 V
=0.25
The difference between the maximum frequency of modulated wave and the minimum frequency of modulated wave is given by
Δf=fmax-fmin
=fc+fm-fc-fm
=2fm
=2(100 MHz)
=2×108 Hz

subject: Physics
q: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
question: If a carrier wave $\mathrm{c}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}$ is amplitude modulated by a modulator signal $\mathrm{m}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{A}$ sin $\omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$ then the equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$ and its modulation index are respectively
solution:
Modulation index
$$
\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{c}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\mathrm{A}}=1
$$
Equation of modulated signal $\left[\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{m}}(\mathrm{t})\right]$
$$
=\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}+\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}}^{\mathrm{t}}
$$
$=\mathrm{A}\left(1+\sin \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{t}\right) \sin \omega_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{t}$
$\left(\right.$ As $\left.\mathrm{E}_{(\mathrm{C})}=\mathrm{A} \sin \omega_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{t}\right)$

subject: Physics
q: A signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> and peak voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Volt</mi><mo> </mo></math>is used to modulate a carrier wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi><mo> </mo></math>and peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Volts</mi></math>. question: A signal of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> and peak voltage of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Volt</mi><mo> </mo></math>is used to modulate a carrier wave of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi><mo> </mo></math>and peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Volts</mi></math>. Choose the correct statement.
options: Modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, side frequency bands are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>.

Modulation index<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, side frequency bands are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>21</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>.

Modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, side frequency bands are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1220</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>1180</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>.

Modulation index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math>, side frequency bands are at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mn>18</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math>.
solution:
Modulation index defines the modulated signal's ratio of maximum to minimum voltage

=VmVc=525=0.2
Carrier Frequency 1.2×103 kHz

fc=1200 kHz. 

Lower side-band frequency, f1=1200-20=1180 KHz. 

Upper band frequency,  f2 =1200+20=1220 KHz .

subject: Physics
q: An audio signal consists of two distinct sounds: one a human speech signal in the frequency band of 200 Hz to 2700 Hz, while the other is a high frequency music signal in the frequency band of 10200 Hz to 15200 Hz. The ratio of the AM signal band width required to send both the signals together to the AM signal band width required to send just the human speech is:
question: An audio signal consists of two distinct sounds: one a human speech signal in the frequency band of 200 Hz to 2700 Hz, while the other is a high frequency music signal in the frequency band of 10200 Hz to 15200 Hz. The ratio of the AM signal band width required to send both the signals together to the AM signal band width required to send just the human speech is:
solution:
Band width for Music signal =15200−10200 =5000 Hz

Band width for speech signal =2700−200=2500Hz

Band with for Both = 2500+5000=7500 Hz

Band with for Human speech =2500 Hz

Required Ratio =75002500=31

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I : A speech signal of 2 kHz is used to modulate a carrier signal of 1 MHz. The bandwidth requirement for the signal is 4 kHz.

Statement II : The side band frequencies are 1002 kHz and 998 kHz. In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
solution:
fm=2 kHz

fc=1 MHz=1000 kHz

Band width =2fm=4 kHz

∴ Side frequencies will be

=fc±fm

=1000±2 kHz

=998 kHz & 1002 kHz

So, statement-I & statement-II both are correct.

subject: Physics
q:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
question:
If the height of transmitting and receiving antennas are 80 m each, the maximum line of sight distance will be :

Given : Earth’s radius =6.4×106 m.
solution:
Given: R=6.4×106 m

Using the relation between the range of the radio communication and the height of the transmission antenna hT and the receiving antenna hR, the maximum line of sight distance between two antennas, is dmax=2RhT+2RhR

⇒dmax=2×2×6.4×106×80=64000 m=64 km

q: The amplitude of upper and lower side bands of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> wave where a carrier signal with frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> sine wave of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> amplitude are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>b</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is_____.
question: The amplitude of upper and lower side bands of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>M</mi></math> wave where a carrier signal with frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>21</mn><mo> </mo><mi>MHz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> peak voltage <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> is amplitude modulated by a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kHz</mi></math> sine wave of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> amplitude are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>b</mi><mn>10</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. Then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is_____.
solution:
In radio communications, a sideband is a band of frequencies higher than or lower than the carrier frequency, that are the result of the modulation process. The signal components above the carrier frequency constitute the upper sideband (USB), and those below the carrier frequency constitute the lower sideband (LSB).

a10=b10=μAC2
⇒ab=1

question: In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AM</mi></math> modulation, a signal is modulated on a carrier wave such that maximum and minimum amplitude are found to be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> respectively. solution:
Given here, Vmax=6 V and Vmin=2 V

Now, modulation index =Vmax-VminVmax+Vmin.×100%

=6-26+2×100%=50%
